title: Blood on Silk
stainChemistry: Classification: Protein. Blood contains hemoglobin—an iron-binding protein that carries oxygen. The heme iron core creates the red color. Silk is itself a protein fiber (fibroin). Protein stains bond to fibers via peptide-to-fiber hydrogen bonds and hydrophobic interactions. Heat denatures the protein, cross-linking it to the substrate and making removal extremely difficult. Treatment: cold water flush to prevent heat-setting, followed by neutral synthetic detergent safe for protein fibers — never enzyme digestants, which dissolve silk fibroin along with the stain.
whyThisWorks: Blood and silk are both protein structures — the stain bonds directly to the fiber. This double-protein problem makes enzyme-based treatments catastrophically wrong: protease enzymes cannot distinguish hemoglobin from silk fibroin and will digest the fiber. NSD lifts blood without attacking silk. Sodium hydrosulfite provides the only safe reducing/bleaching option for protein fibers. Cold water and gentle technique prevent both heat-setting and mechanical fiber damage.
defaultAssumption: Treat as unknown age / possibly dried.

steps: 1. Apply NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) at neutral setting and gently work into the blood stain. Dwell 5–10 minutes. NSD is safe for silk because it won't attack the protein fiber. Never use enzyme-based digestants on silk.

2. Apply sodium hydrosulfite (reducing agent — safe for protein fibers) to break down any remaining blood discoloration. Dwell 5–10 minutes. This is the only safe bleaching option for silk — never use hydrogen peroxide or oxidizing bleach on protein fibers.
3. Flush with NSD to remove all chemical residue. Dwell 3–5 minutes. Rinse with cool water — never use warm or hot water on blood stains or on silk.
4. Apply 28% Acetic acid (diluted 1:10) to neutralize any remaining alkaline residue and restore the fiber's natural state. Dwell 1–2 minutes. Blot dry gently — never wring silk.
products: Sodium hydrosulfite (reducing agent — safe for protein fibers)
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold — heat denatures hemoglobin AND damages silk protein simultaneously. This is the single worst mistake and causes permanent, irreversible staining.
warnings: Using hot water — denatures hemoglobin AND damages silk protein fiber permanently.
Using protease enzymes — DIGESTS silk protein fiber, causing permanent weakening, thinning, and loss of sheen.
Using chlorine bleach — dissolves silk fiber entirely, causing holes in the treated area.
Using high-concentration hydrogen peroxide (>3%) — causes fiber browning, brittleness, and color change on silk.
Scrubbing or rubbing — crushes delicate silk nap, causing permanent shine loss.
NEVER use bleach, chlorine, enzyme, or protease on silk.

steps: 1. Rinse with cold water right away. Blot gently with a clean white cloth — don't rub. Cold water keeps the stain from setting; rubbing pushes it deeper into the fabric.
2. Mix a small squirt of dish soap (Dawn or similar) into cold water. Blot gently onto the stain. Soap breaks down the stain so it lifts off the fabric.
3. Let it sit for 5–10 minutes. Don't use any heat.
4. Blot gently with a clean white cloth until the stain fades.
5. Rinse gently with cold water and lay flat on a towel to air dry — no heat, no wringing.
products: Eucalan Delicate Wash (enzyme-free, safe for silk)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: If the stain persists after two treatment attempts, take it to a professional. Silk is too delicate to risk aggressive home treatments. A professional cleaner has specialized agents that are safe for this material and more effective than household products.
warnings: Test on an inside seam or hidden area first — silk reacts unpredictably to many products.
Never use bleach, enzyme cleaners, or hot water on silk.
Never use hydrogen peroxide on silk — can cause yellowing or discoloration.
Never wring or twist silk — gently press out moisture between towels.

neverDo: Never use hot water — permanently sets blood protein and damages silk simultaneously.
Never use enzyme-based cleaners (protease) — digests silk protein fiber, causing irreversible damage.
Never use chlorine bleach — dissolves silk fiber entirely.
Never use hydrogen peroxide at concentrations above 3% — causes browning and brittleness.
Never scrub or rub — permanently damages silk sheen.
Never use alcohol — permanently sets protein stains.
fiberSensitivities: Silk (fibroin) is a protein fiber — enzyme cleaners digest it the same way they digest the stain.
Silk dyes are often sensitive to both acids and oxidizers — always test on a hidden area.
Silk loses structural integrity with heat, alkalis, and aggressive mechanical action.
Amyl acetate is safe on silk for dry-side components; acetic acid (dilute) is safe for pH restoration.

escalation.when: For persistent blood stains on silk after one treatment cycle, professional dry cleaning is STRONGLY RECOMMENDED. Dry cleaners have access to chelating agents (citrates, EDTA) that can bond to iron deposits without damaging silk, and controlled environments for chemical treatment. Attempting multiple home treatments on delicate silk risks permanent fiber damage, color shift, or brittleness. For antique silk or silk blended with other delicate fibers, call a professional immediately.
escalation.whatToTell: This was a tricky one — blood on silk is one of the more challenging stains because both the stain and the fiber are protein structures. Blood stains set deeper with heat and time. Results depend on how long the stain had been set before treatment — some improvement may continue over the next few days.
escalation.specialistType: silk specialist or wet cleaner with delicate fiber experience

contextQuestions: How old is the stain — was it treated immediately or has it been sitting?
Has the garment been pressed or ironed since the stain occurred?
Is this a printed or dyed silk, and has any product already been applied?
Is this a valuable or antique garment? (escalate immediately if yes)
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Fresh blood on silk responds reasonably well to cold water and gentle dish soap. Any heat exposure prior to treatment dramatically reduces recovery odds. Dyed silk may bleed during treatment — test hidden area first. Antique or blended silk garments should bypass home treatment entirely and go directly to a specialist.

timeEstimate: 10–20 min (professional); 2–3 attempts max at home before escalating
